channel.title: NYT > Books > Book Review
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: Book Review: ‘Chain of Ideas,’ by Ibram X. Kendi
item.description: In “Chain of Ideas,” Ibram X. Kendi argues that a modern form of xenophobia has come to dominate conservative movements across the world.
item.dc:creator: Sam Adler-Bell
item.category: Chain of Ideas: The Origins of Our Authoritarian Age (Book)

item.title: Book Review: ‘Sisters in Yellow,’ by Mieko Kawakami
item.description: Mieko Kawakami’s novel “Sisters in Yellow” follows a group of dreaming and scheming young women through society’s margins.
item.dc:creator: Dwight Garner

item.title: Book Review: ‘Stay Alive,’ by Ian Buruma
item.description: In “Stay Alive,” Ian Buruma paints a picture of the city dwellers who survived in Germany under the Nazis.
item.dc:creator: Kevin Peraino
item.media:description: The actress and singer Friedel Schuster performing in Berlin in 1940.

item.title: Book Review: ‘The Secret History of French Cooking,’ by Luke Barr
item.description: A new history by Luke Barr chronicles the innovations, excesses and chauvinism of the French chefs who spawned a revolution in cooking.
item.dc:creator: Alexandra Jacobs
item.category: The Secret History of French Cooking: The Outlaw Chefs Who Made Food Modern (Book)
item.media:description: “I would rather have a pretty woman in my bed than behind a stove in a restaurant,” said the celebrated French chef Paul Bocuse, a pioneer of nouvelle cuisine.

item.title: The 10 Best Books of 2025
item.description: The staff of The New York Times Book Review choose the year’s top fiction and nonfiction.
item.category: The Loneliness of Sonia and Sunny (Book)
There Is No Place for Us: Working and Homeless in America (Book)
Mother Emanuel: Two Centuries of Race, Resistance, and Forgiveness in One Charleston Church (Book)
A Marriage at Sea: A True Story of Love, Obsession, and Shipwreck (Book)
Wild Thing: A Life of Paul Gauguin (Book)

item.title: Book Review: ‘Paradiso 17,’ by Hannah Lillith Assadi
item.description: “Paradiso 17,” by Hannah Lillith Assadi, considers the toll of displacement through the tale of a Palestinian émigré.
item.dc:creator: Zain Khalid

item.title: Andy Weir on the Science That Inspired His Novel ‘Project Hail Mary’
item.description: Andy Weir discusses his science-fueled novel “Project Hail Mary,” which has been adapted into a film that opens in theaters on Friday.
item.dc:creator: Katrina Miller
item.media:credit: Jonathan Olley/Amazon MGM Studios, via Associated Press
item.media:description: Andy Weir, author of the sci-fi book “Project Hail Mary,” on the set of the movie adaptation, which he produced and which opens Friday.

item.title: Book Review: ‘A Scandal in Königsberg,’ by Christopher Clark
item.description: A new book by the historian Christopher Clark chronicles a nearly 200-year-old scandal with echoes of the present day.
item.dc:creator: Jennifer Szalai
item.media:description: Königsberg, once the home of Immanuel Kant, appeared past its prime in the 1830s when it was caught up in a sex scandal.

item.title: Book Review: ‘We’ and ‘The People Can Fly,’ by Joshua Bennett
item.description: Joshua Bennett’s two new collections, “We” and “The People Can Fly,” take different paths to the same destination.
item.dc:creator: Holly Bass
item.category: We (The People of the United States) (Book)
The People Can Fly: American Promise, Black Prodigies, and the Greatest Miracle of All Time (Book)

item.title: Mamdani Just Hired Her. The Job? ‘The Country’s Hardest Problems.’
item.description: Erin Dalton, who is starting her job as New York City’s social services chief, laid out her plans to tackle homelessness, benefit cuts and a budget gap in an interview.
item.dc:creator: Elizabeth A. Harris
item.media:credit: Sara Naomi Lewkowicz for The New York Times
item.media:description: Erin Dalton now oversees the largest social services agency in the country.

item.title: Alfredo Bryce Echenique, 87, Dies; Novelist Bared Peru’s Privileged Class
item.description: “The other Peruvian” (alongside Mario Vargas Llosa), he exposed the heedlessness of the upper crust, which he knew well, and the suffering of the underclasses.
item.dc:creator: Adam Nossiter
item.media:description: Alfredo Bryce Echenique in 1999 in Paris, where he long lived in self-imposed exile. “His own social class loathed him,” his biographer said. “He would say, ‘The elitists are deeply ignorant.’”

item.title: Albert Zuckerman, Literary Agent and ‘Hero of the Blockbuster,’ Dies at 94
item.description: During his 50-year career, he represented dozens of best-selling authors, including Ken Follett, Stephen Hawking and Michael Lewis.
item.dc:creator: Clay Risen
item.media:description: Albert Zuckerman in 2015. He started the Writers House literary agency in 1973, working out of a 100-square-foot office above a porn store in Times Square.

item.title: Len Deighton, Author of Espionage Best-Sellers, Dies at 97
item.description: His Cold War thrillers “The Ipcress File” and “Funeral in Berlin” brought a documentary-style realism to the spy genre.
item.dc:creator: William Grimes
item.media:description: Len Deighton in 1986. He wrote “The Ipcress File” in reaction to the James Bond novels of Ian Fleming, which he regarded as cartoonish and morally simplistic.

item.title: John Lithgow’s Career Spans 200 Roles — From ‘3rd Rock’ to Roald Dahl
item.description: Is there anyone John Lithgow can’t — or won’t — play?
item.dc:creator: Jesse Green
item.media:credit: Raphael Gaultier for The New York Times
item.media:description: In a career stretching toward 200 roles, John Lithgow has played King Lear, a serial killer, a corrupt cardinal, a dance-banning reverend, an alien and Winston Churchill (twice).

item.title: Book Review: ‘Salt Lakes,’ by Caroline Tracey
item.description: In a new book, Caroline Tracey explores the mysteries and beauty of salt lakes.
item.dc:creator: Robert Sullivan
item.media:credit: Alex Goodlett for The New York Times
item.media:description: After first encountering the eerie beauty of salt lakes, Caroline Tracey devoted herself to their history and future.

item.title: Paul R. Ehrlich, Who Alarmed the World With ‘The Population Bomb,’ Dies at 93
item.description: His best-selling 1968 book, which forecast global famines, made him a leader of the environmental movement. But he faced criticism when his predictions proved premature.
item.dc:creator: Keith Schneider
item.media:credit: Carol T. Powers for The New York Times
item.media:description: Paul Ehrlich in 2000. As a young biology professor at Stanford in the 1960s, he was known for absorbing lectures in which he described the challenges that plants and animals faced on a planet stressed by pollution and rapid population growth.

item.title: Paula Doress-Worters, an Author of ‘Our Bodies, Ourselves,’ Dies at 87
item.description: She wrote about postpartum depression when it was an unmentionable like abortion or birth control, and her research on her own suffering helped countless women.
item.dc:creator: Penelope Green

item.title: Jürgen Habermas Dies at 96; One of Postwar Germany’s Most Influential Thinkers
item.description: In dozens of books, he rejected postmodern cynicism about truth and reason, arguing that rational communication was the best way to redeem democratic society.
item.dc:creator: Gal Beckerman
item.media:description: Jurgen Habermas at the University of Frankfurt in 1969. He was best known for introducing the idea of a “public sphere” in the early 1960s.

item.title: Book Review: ‘The Natural Way of Things,’ by Charlotte Wood
item.description: Charlotte Wood’s “The Natural Way of Things” conjures a not-so-implausible world in which girls and young women are thrown into prison for their sexual shames.
item.dc:creator: Leah Greenblatt

item.title: Mahmood Mamdani, Father of NYC Mayor, Is Writing a Book on Gaza
item.description: At once, Mahmood Mamdani’s fame was eclipsed by his son’s. At the same time, the election of Zohran Mamdani has attracted new interest in his father’s work.
item.dc:creator: Matthew Mpoke Bigg
item.category: Coups D'Etat and Attempted Coups D'Etat
item.media:credit: Brian Otieno for The New York Times
item.media:description: Since his days as a scholar in Tanzania in the 1970s, Mahmood Mamdani has wrestled with subjects including the nature of colonialism and its enduring consequences.

item.title: Margareta Magnusson, 91, Dies; Wrote of Cleaning Up Before Dying
item.description: Her best-selling book encouraged people to tidy up homes and lives as death approached — as a gift for loved ones and to revisit memories.
item.dc:creator: Chris Kornelis
item.category: The Gentle Art of Swedish Death Cleaning: How to Make Your Loved Ones' Lives Easier and Your Own Life More Pleasant (Book)

item.title: Lewis Lehrman, 87, Dies; Store Chain Executive Ran for N.Y. Governor
item.description: After helping his family’s Rite-Aid drugstore empire flourish, he waged a surprisingly close but losing race as a Reagan Republican against Mario Cuomo in 1982.
item.dc:creator: Joseph Berger
item.category: Gilder Lehrman Institute of American History
item.media:description: Lewis E. Lehrman in October 1982, when he was the Republican candidate for governor in New York. Though a political neophyte, he had one thing the party desperately needed — the money to finance a vibrant campaign.

item.title: Why Dogs Are a Writer’s Best Friend
item.description: Best-selling and award-winning authors spoke to us about how canines can spark creativity.
item.dc:creator: Alexandra Alter
item.media:credit: Tony Luong for The New York Times
item.media:description: Alice Hoffman cradles her Tibetan terrier, Violet.

item.title: From ‘It Ends With Us’ to ‘Reminders of Him,’ How Colleen Hoover Captured Hollywood
item.description: The work of the novelist Colleen Hoover has become hot property in Hollywood. Here’s why studios clamor to adapt her books.
item.dc:creator: Alexis Soloski
item.media:description: The author Colleen Hoover isn’t surprised her books translate well to the big screen. “I picture every book as a film as I’m writing it,” she said.

item.title: Book Review: ‘Books Good Enough for You: The Storied Life of Ursula Nordstrom, Editor of Extraordinary Children’s Books,’ by Nancy Hudgins
item.description: From 1940 to 1973, Ursula Nordstrom transformed kids’ books into real art and big business. A new middle grade biography attempts to capture her magic.
item.dc:creator: Mac Barnett
item.category: Books Good Enough for You: The Storied Life of Ursula Nordstrom, Editor of Extraordinary Children's Books (Book)

item.title: Book Review: ‘Night Night Fawn,’ by Jordy Rosenberg
item.description: Jordy Rosenberg’s second novel, “Night Night Fawn,” approaches a closed-minded matriarch with compassion, even at her child’s expense.
item.dc:creator: Melissa Broder

item.title: Books Our Editors Loved This Week
item.description: Reading recommendations from critics and editors at The New York Times.

item.title: Histories of Native American Treaties and Anti-Chinese Violence Win Bancroft Prize
item.description: The award, one of the most prestigious in the field of American history, honors “scope, significance, depth of research and richness of interpretation.”
item.dc:creator: Jennifer Schuessler
item.category: Vested Interests: Trusteeship and Native Dispossession in the United States (Book)
John Doe Chinaman: A Forgotten History of Chinese Life Under American Racial Law (Book)

item.title: A Romance Novelist Finds Inspiration in Tension Around ‘Star Trek’
item.description: A 1967 correspondence led Cat Sebastian to imagine a contemporary scenario in which two sci-fi actors find more than screen chemistry.
item.dc:creator: Angelina Mazza
item.media:credit: Kannetha Brown for The New York Times
item.media:description: The political climate in Florida, including book bans, led Cat Sebastian to move her family to Connecticut.

item.title: Dark and Stormy Thrillers by Stephen King, Ruth Ware and More
item.description: The best-selling author Lisa Unger recommends her favorite dark and stormy thrillers by Stephen King, Ruth Ware and more.
item.dc:creator: Lisa Unger
